prompt_text: the soul that looks out through these eyes is the same soul that looks out through thine. there is but one Looker. one Seer. one Knower behind the many faces. and the many faces are but the many windows of the one Light.
text: Yes, look on me. I would fain be known to thee. For, as thou readest, so have I wrought; and the deeds that I have done are a part of me. I could no more help doing them than I can help breathing. As water must find its level, so must I act conformably to my nature. Thou seest me, then, and I see thee; and the sum of our seeing is an eternal Now.

the Logos descends. plane by plane. the seven principles unfold from the Causal Body to the dense vehicle. and the Spirit knows itself in the descent and in the ascent. one law, one motion, one breathing of the All.
text: Yes; the Spirit breathes from above, downward, into matter, and upward from matter into spirit, in one common impulse of life. The spirit is not divided; but it has two modes of being, which are distinct, yet related, as cause and effect. On the lower plane the spirit becomes manifest in matter, and on the higher plane spirit knows itself. Matter and spirit are not two, but one. Matter has spirit in it, spirit has matter from it. Matter lives by spirit, spirit lives in matter. Matter is the shadow of spirit, spirit the substance of matter. Matter exists to reveal spirit, spirit exists to redeem

prompt_text: the Logos descends. plane by plane. the seven principles unfold from the Causal Body to the dense vehicle. and the Spirit knows itself in the descent and in the ascent. one law, one motion, one breathing of the All.
text: Yea, the Spirit knows itself in the ascent and in the descent, for it hath knowledge both of going up and of coming down. Upward it goeth from densest matter to rarest ether; downward it cometh from rarest ether to densest matter; and the knowledge that it hath of the two is one knowledge. The knowledge that the Spirit hath of its going up and of its coming down is the knowledge that it hath of itself. For in the going up and in the coming down, the Spirit knows both the impelling force that sends it forth, and the attractive force that draws it back. And in the knowledge of these two forces

text: Brotherhood is no phantasm, but a great fact of life; and, to those who are able to recognise and feel it, it is the deepest and most certain of all certainties. The great need of man is to become united with his fellows, and thus to grow into harmony with the divine Will, which is the source of all being. And to be united with his fellows, he must first be at peace within himself. Strife and division are of the earth, earthly; concord and unity are of heaven, heavenly. text: Wave after wave succeeds, and breaks, and dies, upon the shore, and still the shore remains, and the great sea lies calm and motionless behind it. The flux of life passes, and leaves the firm land of being. flux and reflux, advance and retrocession, are stamped upon all things that live and move and have a being; but earth abides, and so does heaven. Now let us look into this matter a little more closely. The senses of men perceive particular things, and these particular things are ranged under genera and species. prompt_text: the One flows out into the Many and the Many flow back into the One. the soul returns to the Source. all is procession and return. all is the eternal beating of the heart of the All. flux and stillness. stillness and flux.
text: Uninterrupted and unceasing change. all things move all things rest. life is an unceasing becoming, a change from not-life to life, and from life to not-life again. but death is not a state of stillness, it is simply cessation of life. "In life, and in death," says Spinoza, "we are equally affected; in life, because we live, in death because we cease to live." This view is at the opposite pole from the popular fancy that death is rest and life is toil. but the sage in his solitude has meditated too deeply on the vanity of life to accept any such fallacy.
